Underground nuclear test program, FY 1973 : environmental impact statement.

The U.S. Atomic Energy Commission proposed an underground nuclear testing program at the Nevada Test Site in Nye County, Nevada, about 65 miles northwest of Las Vegas. The program aimed to develop nuclear weapons, test nuclear effects for the Department of Defense, and explore peaceful uses of nuclear explosives. The tests were planned to be conducted underground to minimize environmental impact, with improved containment designs to prevent radioactive releases. The program was expected to have minimal adverse effects on the environment, with some potential for minor structural damage in nearby communities.
Nevada Test Site, Nye County, Nevada

The underground nuclear test program was proposed during a time when the U.S. was heavily invested in nuclear weapons development, particularly in the context of the Cold War. There was some public concern about the environmental and health impacts of nuclear testing, but the government maintained that underground tests would be safer and less harmful than above-ground tests. Ultimately, the program proceeded as planned, with numerous tests conducted at the Nevada Test Site.
"The sensitivity of aquatic organisms to radiation is poorly understood and relatively unstudied." — Fish Commission of Oregon
"The collection and publication of this material should give the public a much better understanding of the testing program's objectives and activities." — Arizona Game & Fish Department
